FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'We analyzed data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study of 9 to 12-year-old adolescents in the United States' (abstract, methods section confirms ABCD 4.0 release, Years 0-1)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific smartphone/SM variables: texting, video chat, social media, videos (YouTube), and video games as separate exposures. Text states 'contemporary screen modalities (eg, television, video games, text, video chat, social media)' with separate coefficients for each modality.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking screen time modalities to MIND diet nutrition score. Text: 'Each additional hour per day of screen time at year 0 and year 1 in the form of watching videos ([−0.18, 95% CI:−0.24, −0.12]; [−0.24, 95% CI:−0.30, −0.19]), video games ([−0.15, 95% CI:−0.21, −0.09]; [−0.16, 95% CI:−0.22, −0.10]), and television ([−0.14, 95% CI:−0.20, −0.07]; [−0.07, 95% CI:−0.14, −0.01]) was associated with a lower nutrition score at year 1. Texting (−0.15, 95% CI:−0.24, −0.05) and social media (−0.16, 95% CI:−0.28, −0.05) screen use at year 1 were associated with lower nutrition scores' (Results).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients (β) reported for all screen modalities with 95% CIs and p-values. Table 2 displays full regression results with B coefficients and confidence intervals for prospective and cross-sectional analyses.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data: 'This study presents a prospective and cross-sectional analysis of data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study' with both prospective (year 0 to year 1) and cross-sectional (year 1) analyses conducted.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Academic Pediatrics, 2024), not a preprint or duplicate. DOI: 10.1016/j.acap.2024.01.023

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes cross-sectional analyses with screen time as independent variable predicting nutrition outcomes (non-SM outcome). EC7 does not apply because paper reports screen time → nutrition associations (not demographic-only longitudinal prediction of SM use).
notes: E8 applied: Inferential tests (regression with p-values and confidence intervals) are clearly reported, not descriptive statistics only.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'We analyzed cross-sectional data from Year 3 of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive DevelopmentSM Study (ABCD Study®, N = 9859, 2019–2021, mostly 12–13-years-old)' in a nationally representative U.S. sample.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific smartphone/SM variables: 'problematic use of video games, social media, and mobile phones' plus recreational screen time for 'television/movies, video games, texting, social media, and the internet' as separate modalities.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking transgender/gender-questioning identity to screen use modalities and problematic use measures. Text: 'transgender adolescents reported 4.51 (95% CI 1.17–7.85) more hours of total daily recreational screen time including more time on television/movies, video games, texting, social media, and the internet, compared to cisgender adolescents' and results show associations with SMAQ, VGAQ, and MPIQ scores.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients (B), 95% confidence intervals, and p-values reported for all screen modalities and problematic use measures. Table 2 displays regression results with 11 unadjusted and 11 adjusted models.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis: 'We conducted a cross-sectional analysis of the Year 3 follow-up of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study' with multiple linear regression models analyzing associations.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Annals of Epidemiology, 2024), not a preprint or duplicate. DOI: 10.1016/j.annepidem.2024.04.013

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports cross-sectional associations where screen time is outcome variable but with individual psychological/demographic predictors. EC7 does not apply because: (1) cross-sectional analysis is included, and (2) predictor is demographic/identity variable (transgender/gender-questioning status), which is a sociodemographic characteristic.
notes: E14 applied: Sociodemographic predictors (gender identity) of screen/SM use in cross-sectional context. MET because cross-sectional analysis included.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'We analyzed prospective cohort data from 9,398 early adolescents aged 11-12 years (48.4% female, 45% racial/ethnic minority) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Years 2-3, 2018-2021).'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific bedtime smartphone/SM variables: 'talking on the phone or texting, listening to music, and using social media' plus watching TV/videos, playing video games, and browsing internet during bedtime. All measured separately with specific frequencies.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression analyses (logistic, ordinal logistic, and linear) linking bedtime screen use to sleep outcomes (duration and disturbance). Text: 'Talking/texting on the phone, listening to music, and using social media were all prospectively associated with shorter sleep duration, greater overall sleep disturbance, and a higher factor score for disorders of initiating and maintaining sleep one year later' with reported ORs, B coefficients, and p-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric statistics reported: odds ratios (OR), B coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values. Table 3 displays comprehensive regression results for all bedtime screen use exposures and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis: 'We analyzed prospective cohort data from 9,398 early adolescents...in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Years 2-3, 2018-2021)' examining associations between Year 2 exposures and Year 3 outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024), not a preprint or duplicate. DOI: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2024.06.006

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports screen/SM as independent variable predicting non-SM outcomes (sleep duration and disturbance). EC7 does not apply because screen time is the predictor, not the outcome.
notes: E3 not applicable (no latent constructs). E8 applied: Inferential tests (logistic regression, ordinal logistic regression, linear regression with p-values and CIs) clearly reported for all associations, not descriptive statistics only.
